According to the news agency URA.ru, on the evening of 5 November in [Yekaterinburg](/wiki/Yekaterinburg "Yekaterinburg"), police detained a resident, Daniil Kornev, on whose car there was a sticker, privately made by the townspeople, with the well-known aphorism of Alexei Navalny "United Russia is a party of crooks and thieves" and an image of a bear with a bag in its mouth against the backdrop of a map of Russia. An administrative offense report was drawn up against him (Part 1 of Article 5.12 of the [Code of the Russian Federation on Administrative Offenses](/wiki/Code_of_the_Russian_Federation_on_Administrative_Offenses "Code of the Russian Federation on Administrative Offenses") "Production, distribution or placement of campaign materials in violation of election legislation"), from which it follows the sticker is campaigning for United Russia not paid for from the election fund, and a subpoena was served. In [Pervouralsk](/wiki/Pervouralsk "Pervouralsk"), local businessman Vitaly Listratkin was fined 1,000 rubles for distributing forty mugs with a statement about United Russia. On 31 October, at the office of the Parnassus branch in Yekaterinburg, law enforcement officers seized a batch of stickers with Navalny's phrase and a computer.[[508]](#cite_note-511)

On 6 November 2011, journalist and blogger Oleg Kozyrev discovered an almost complete "confusing" similarity between the campaign materials of United Russia and the Moscow Electoral Commission.[[509]](#cite_note-512) Subsequently, it turned out that under the slogan "Moscow for life, for people" and the design accompanying it, there was a simultaneous advertising campaign, firstly, by the Moscow government, secondly, by the Moscow City Electoral Committee, and thirdly, by the United Russia party.[[510]](#cite_note-513) The [Central Election Commission of the Russian Federation](/wiki/Central_Election_Commission_(Russia) "Central Election Commission (Russia)") and the Moscow City Electoral Committee did not find any contradictions to the electoral legislation in that the election billboards of the United Russia party are similar to the Moscow City Electoral Committee's public service announcements dedicated to the December elections.[[511]](#cite_note-514) A similar point of view was expressed by Senator Valery Ryazansky, member of the Bureau of the Supreme Council of the United Russia party, on [Echo of Moscow](/wiki/Echo_of_Moscow "Echo of Moscow") radio:

> … yes, there is some similarity, as they say, but I say again that per the law, the party that decides to hang billboards coordinates their sketches with a mandatory commission. Therefore, there can be no claims against United Russia.

— [[512]](#cite_note-515)

Also on 6 November 2011, a video appeared on the Internet with information that in [Murmansk](/wiki/Murmansk "Murmansk"), United Russia members concluded agreements for "self-agitation" at 1,500 rubles per vote given to the party and 250 rubles per brought friend.[[513]](#cite_note-516)[[514]](#cite_note-517) On 23 November 2011, *Novaya Gazeta* published an article about the correspondence between United Russia, the department for the media, press, broadcasting, and mass communications of the [Krasnodar Krai](/wiki/Krasnodar_Krai "Krasnodar Krai"), Internet trolls, and regional officials, from which it follows that pro-United Russia comments in the [blogosphere](/wiki/Blogosphere "Blogosphere") are most often written by officials and deputies for money, and that United Russia is conducting its election campaign in the Kuban with a blunt and blatant use of [administrative resources](/wiki/Administrative_resources "Administrative resources"). The election posters of the ruling party, which are hung all over the country, are pasted during working hours by the same officials who are united by Internet mailings conducted by the Kuban media department on instructions from Moscow.[[515]](#cite_note-518)

On 23 November 2011, *Gazeta.ru* published an article with a transcript of a closed speech by Alexander Aksyonov, head of the [Sokolinaya Gora District](/wiki/Sokolinaya_Gora_District "Sokolinaya Gora District") Council in Moscow. The transcript explains in detail how the plan to collect votes for United Russia should be carried out. Aksyonov demands the heads of enterprises collect absentee certificates from their subordinates and hand them over to officials so that United Russia can gain 58% in the elections in Moscow (even though according to a closed Foundation "Public Opinion" poll conducted at the start of the campaign and published in *Gazeta.ru*, United Russia had a rating of 29% in Moscow).[[516]](#cite_note-519) According to RIA Novosti, the Moscow City Electoral Committee stated that the information contained in the article "is unfounded" and that the audio recording raises doubts about its authenticity.[[517]](#cite_note-520)

On 21 December 2011, a member of the regional political council of United Russia, [Vladimir Semago](/wiki/Vladimir_Semago "Vladimir Semago"), appeared in *Novaya Gazeta* with an article titled "This is not a falsification of the election results, but a conspiracy to forcibly retain power". In the article, he stated that a group of conspirators created a criminal community to rig parliamentary elections to retain power. The community included the CEC headed by [Vladimir Churov](/wiki/Vladimir_Churov "Vladimir Churov") and the chairmen of grassroots election commissions. Similar communities were created at the regional level in executive power structures. The conspirators' activities received support from the FSB, the Ministry of Internal Affairs and, most likely, were coordinated at the very top. Such actions fall under articles 210 and 278 of the Criminal Code of the Russian Federation and are punishable by imprisonment from 10 to 20 years.[[518]](#cite_note-521)

Scientists from the Medical University of Vienna have proposed a method for assessing the presence of electoral fraud. As a result, they proved that there were falsifications in the elections of the Russian President in 2012 and to the State Duma of the Russian Federation in 2011; in particular, ballot stuffing for some candidates. The article appeared in the journal *[Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Proceedings_of_the_National_Academy_of_Sciences_of_the_United_States_of_America "Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America")* and is available in the public domain.[[519]](#cite_note-522)[[520]](#cite_note-523)

## Notable departures from the party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Russia&action=edit&section=96 "Edit section: Notable departures from the party")]

Former co-chairman of the Party Supreme Council [Yury Luzhkov](/wiki/Yury_Luzhkov "Yury Luzhkov"), who left the party simultaneously with a resignation from the post of mayor of Moscow, speaking on 21 October 2010 as a dean of the [International University in Moscow](/wiki/International_University_in_Moscow "International University in Moscow") with the lecture "Development of Moscow and the Renaissance of Russia" and answering questions from the audience, said that he was always wary of United Russia since there were no democratic institutions. "The irony of fate: I am one of the founding fathers of the United Russia party. My attitude towards it has always been warily critical." "I always told the party chairman Boris Gryzlov that we have no discussions. We always and in everything obey the [administration](/wiki/Presidential_Administration_of_Russia "Presidential Administration of Russia")." "When the 122 federal law on the monetization of benefits was adopted, there were two thousand comments to it; we accepted it without discussion. Then there was the popular phrase: Duma is no place for discussions. You can not do it this way!". "The party is a servant, and I left it," Luzhkov concluded.[[521]](#cite_note-524)

Other famous personalities that left the party include State Duma deputy (2000–2016) and ex-governor of the [Krasnoyarsk Krai](/wiki/Krasnoyarsk_Krai "Krasnoyarsk Krai") [Valery Zubov](/wiki/Valery_Zubov "Valery Zubov"), State Duma deputy (1999–2005) and ex-governor of the [Chelyabinsk Oblast](/wiki/Chelyabinsk_Oblast "Chelyabinsk Oblast") [Mikhail Yurevich](/wiki/Mikhail_Yurevich "Mikhail Yurevich"), ex-head of [Khakassia](/wiki/Khakassia "Khakassia") [Aleksey Lebed](/wiki/Aleksey_Lebed "Aleksey Lebed"), ballerina and actress [Anastasia Volochkova](/wiki/Anastasia_Volochkova "Anastasia Volochkova"), farmer and politician Zhoakim Krima, writer Yevgeny Kasimov, General [Yevgeny Yuryev](/wiki/Yevgeny_Yuryev "Yevgeny Yuryev"), former State Duma deputies [Vladimir Semago](/wiki/Vladimir_Semago "Vladimir Semago") and Anatoly Yermolin, director of the Lenin State Farm CJSC and, at the time of leaving the party, the operating deputy of the [Moscow Oblast Duma](/wiki/Moscow_Oblast_Duma "Moscow Oblast Duma") [Pavel Grudinin](/wiki/Pavel_Grudinin "Pavel Grudinin"), former State Duma deputy [Igor Morozov](/wiki/Igor_Morozov_(politician) "Igor Morozov (politician)"), Deputy Prime Minister of the Irkutsk Region Alexander Bitarov, former head of the Bolshaya Okhta municipality of St. Petersburg Nikolai Payalin, ex-head of the administration of several St. Petersburg regions and brother of politician [Arkady Dvorkovich](/wiki/Arkady_Dvorkovich "Arkady Dvorkovich") Mikhail Dvorkovich, and cousin of Vladimir Putin [Igor Putin](/wiki/Igor_Putin "Igor Putin").[[522]](#cite_note-525)

In addition to criminal grounds in some cases (see below), for political reasons, the Governor of the [Stavropol Krai](/wiki/Stavropol_Krai "Stavropol Krai") [Alexander Chernogorov](/wiki/Alexander_Chernogorov "Alexander Chernogorov"), the mayor of [Volgograd](/wiki/Volgograd "Volgograd") [Roman Grebennikov](/wiki/Roman_Grebennikov "Roman Grebennikov"), and mayor of [Tula](/wiki/Tula,_Russia "Tula, Russia") Alisa Tolkacheva were excluded from the party.[[523]](#cite_note-526)

### Mass departures from the party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Russia&action=edit&section=97 "Edit section: Mass departures from the party")]

In 2015, almost a hundred people in [Kopeysk](/wiki/Kopeysk "Kopeysk") left United Russia. All employees who left the party worked at three Kopeysk enterprises: Gorvodokanal CJSC, Kopeysk Treatment Plant LLC, and Kopeysk Water Supply Networks LLC. The employees of these enterprises have not been paid salaries for a long time since the organizations do not have the funds for this. Employees blamed the head of Kopeysk, Vyacheslav Istomin, for the lack of money. According to the employees of the enterprises, their complaints about the situation were ignored. This prompted them to write a statement about leaving the party.[[524]](#cite_note-527)

In 2021, in the [Altai Krai](/wiki/Altai_Krai "Altai Krai") village of Stepnoye, more than 120 employees of the Kuchuksulfat plant simultaneously left the United Russia party. According to these employees, because of the current situation with the plant, they are worried about the possible change of the owner of the enterprise and afraid of a raider seizure of the enterprise, and they do not see any actions from the party to help resolve the issue.[[525]](#cite_note-528)[[526]](#cite_note-529)[[527]](#cite_note-530)

## Sanctions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Russia&action=edit&section=98 "Edit section: Sanctions")]

On 16 December 2022, due to [Russia's invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), United Russia was included in the [EU sanctions list](/wiki/European_Union_sanctions "European Union sanctions"). According to the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), the party supported Russia's aggressive war against Ukraine and the annexation of Ukrainian territories; thus, the party is responsible for supporting actions that undermine or threaten Ukraine's territorial integrity and independence.[[528]](#cite_note-dw.com-531)[[529]](#cite_note-532) All party assets in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") are to be frozen, and any member of these parties are to be banned from entering the EU.[[528]](#cite_note-dw.com-531) On 24 February 2023, United Russia was included in the sanctions list of Canada.[[530]](#cite_note-533)

## Leading members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_Russia&action=edit&section=99 "Edit section: Leading members")]

Notable members of the party may be found in [category:United Russia politicians](/wiki/Category:United_Russia_politicians "Category:United Russia politicians").

* [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") – [President of Russia](/wiki/President_of_Russia "President of Russia") and former chairman of the party
* [Boris Gryzlov](/wiki/Boris_Gryzlov "Boris Gryzlov") – former [Interior Minister](/wiki/Ministry_of_Internal_Affairs_(Russia) "Ministry of Internal Affairs (Russia)") and Chairman of the Supreme Council of the United Russia and former leader of the party
* [Vyacheslav Volodin](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Volodin "Vyacheslav Volodin") – current [Chairman of the State Duma](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_State_Duma "Chairman of the State Duma")
* [Valentina Matviyenko](/wiki/Valentina_Matviyenko "Valentina Matviyenko") – current [Chairwoman of the Federation Council](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Federation_Council_(Russia) "Chairman of the Federation Council (Russia)")
* [Sergey Shoygu](/wiki/Sergey_Shoygu "Sergey Shoygu") – former [Defence Minister](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(Russia) "Ministry of Defence (Russia)"), former [Emergency Minister](/wiki/Ministry_of_Emergency_Situations_(Russia) "Ministry of Emergency Situations (Russia)"), former leader of [Unity](/wiki/Unity_(Russian_political_party) "Unity (Russian political party)") party and former leader of the party
* [Mintimer Shaymiev](/wiki/Mintimer_Shaymiev "Mintimer Shaymiev") – [President of Tatarstan](/wiki/President_of_Tatarstan "President of Tatarstan") until 2010
* [Vladislav Surkov](/wiki/Vladislav_Surkov "Vladislav Surkov") – former First Deputy Chief of Staff of the President
* [Alexander Zhukov](/wiki/Alexander_Zhukov_(politician,_born_1956) "Alexander Zhukov (politician, born 1956)") – [First Vice Chairman of the State Duma](/wiki/List_of_Deputy_Chairmen_of_the_State_Duma "List of Deputy Chairmen of the State Duma") and former [Deputy Prime Minister](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_Russia "Deputy Prime Minister of Russia")
* [Dmitry Medvedev](/wiki/Dmitry_Medvedev "Dmitry Medvedev") – current chairman of the party, former [Prime Minister of Russia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Russia "Prime Minister of Russia"), former President of Russia and the leader of the party's Federal list to the Duma (since 24 September 2011)
* [Denis Pushilin](/wiki/Denis_Pushilin "Denis Pushilin") and [Leonid Pasechnik](/wiki/Leonid_Pasechnik "Leonid Pasechnik") – leaders of the self-proclaimed [Donetsk People's Republic](/wiki/Donetsk_People%27s_Republic "Donetsk People's Republic") and [Luhansk People's Republic](/wiki/Luhansk_People%27s_Republic "Luhansk People's Republic")[[531]](#cite_note-534)
* [Ruslan Edelgeriev](/wiki/Ruslan_Edelgeriev "Ruslan Edelgeriev") – Chairman of the Government of the Chechen Republic from 24 May 2012 to 25 June 2018.[[532]](#cite_note-535)

[ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781317989943](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781317989943 "Special:BookSources/9781317989943"). Retrieved 22 August 2016. "The party of power in Russia has not achieved ... single-minded mastery of the power and wealth associated with the control of patronage. The party is united only in its support for and dependence on the Kremlin; it is divided when its principal clients take opposing sides. ... United Russia is not a programmatic party, but a mechanism for extracting rents and distributing patronage. ... In Russia, the party is the creature of the presidency. ... [T]he construction of a lasting party of power such as united Russia requires a sustained commitment on the part of the authorities, one which president Putin has been willing to undertake. ... [T]he concerted effort by President Putin's administration to build up a lasting party of power is a significant development in post-1993 Russian politics ... ."
65. **[^](#cite_ref-66)** Moraski, Bryon J. (2013). [*The Duma's electoral system: Lessons in endogeneity*](https://books.google.com/books?id=yGOpAgAAQBAJ). Routledge. p. 109. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781136641022](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781136641022 "Special:BookSources/9781136641022"). Retrieved 22 August 2016. "With the March 2000 election of President Vladimir Putin, ... the suspicion was that ... institutional changes 'could resurrect a system dominated by a single "party of power"' (McFaul 2000, 30). ... Still, Russia's electoral system remained largely unchanged for the 2003 Duma election, although the results certainly fuelled speculation that a dominant-party-state had begun to emerge. ... With the union of Fatherland-All Russia and Unity, Russia's party of power had changed once again, this time emerging as United Russia. The 2003 Duma election provided some evidence that the electoral system was working in the party of power's favour."

388. **[^](#cite_ref-391)** According to the company's statements, as of 14 February 2013, 80.57% in the authorized capital of the joint-stock company and its 89.59% shares belong to TNS Energo Group of Companies LLC. In turn, 100% of the authorized capital of TNS Energo belongs to the offshore company Sunflake Limited registered in Cyprus. As follows from the official report of YaSK OJSC for the 4th quarter of 2012, earlier Cypriot offshore companies owned 50.14% of the company's authorized capital. ([«Единую Россию» подключили к счётчику через Кипр](https://ryb.ru/2013/03/28/29382) // Рыбинск онлайн, 28.3.2013). According to Article 30 of the Federal Law on Political Parties:
     > 3. Donations to a political party and its regional branches are not allowed from:
     >
     > d) Russian legal entities with foreign participation, if the share (contribution) of foreign participation in their authorized (share) capital exceeds 30 percent on the day the donation is made (for open joint-stock companies - on the day the list of persons entitled to participate in the annual general meeting of shareholders for the previous financial year)

     — Federal Law on Political Parties No. 95-FZ of 11 July 2001
